title: Fed holds interest rates steady for third straight meeting, as Powell vows to remain as governor
author: Sigma Capital
metaDescription: # Fed holds interest rates steady for third straight meeting, as Powell vows to remain as governor
excerpt: # Fed holds interest rates steady for third straight meeting, as Powell vows to remain as governor

content.introduction: # Fed holds interest rates steady for third straight meeting, as Powell vows to remain as governor
body:
Updated on: April 29, 2026 / 6:04 PM EDT
/ CBS News

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said on Wednesday that he plans to remain as a board governor after his term ends in May, announcing the move after the central bank again left its benchmark interest rate unchanged amid [rising inflation](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/cpi-report-today-march-2026-inflation-iran-war-trump/) due to the [Iran war](https://chartbeat.com/publishing/dashboard/cbsnews.com/?sortColumns=engaged_time::pageviews_today#path=/live-updates/iran-war-trump-warning-strait-of-hormuz-bab-el-mandeb-threat-oil-prices/).

Powell had previously said he would stay on as Fed chair until the Department of Justice ended an investigation into his oversight of renovations of the Fed's Washington, D.C., headquarters, calling the inquiry politically motivated. Jeanine Pirro, U.S. Attorney for the District of Columbia, said on April 24 that her office [would end the probe](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/justice-department-drops-probe-into-fed-chair-jerome-powell/) into Powell.
"I'm waiting for the investigation to be well and truly over with transparency and finality," Powell said on Wednesday when asked in a press conference when he would leave his post as a Fed governor.
It is the first time since 1948 that a Fed chief has opted to carry on as a board governor after his term has ended, according to Capital Economics.

Powell added that he is concerned about the Fed's ability to maintain its independence amid political challenges, such as President Trump's attempt to fire Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook. The Supreme Court is expected to [issue a ruling](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/supreme-court-lisa-cook-fed-trump-oral-arguments/) later this year on whether the president has the authority to remove a Federal Reserve official.
"The institution is battered — we have had to go to the courts," Powell said. "It's not over."
